sentences.text: My name is Norbert Kerenyi.
I was born Norbert Kohn in Hungary in January 15, 1928.
In Budapest.
My family-- my father and my mother both-- were born outside of Budapest.
And when I was born, our family already had two children.
I had an older brother and an older sister.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After me, in 1929, we had one more member of the family who is my younger brother.
So for all intents and purposes, I was a middle child.
My older brother's name was Gabor, and he was born in 1924, April.
And my sister was-- her name is Idit, and she was born in 1925, August.
And my younger brother, Ervin, was born in 1929, September, and I think I told you his name is Ervin.
I would rather tell you which was not.
Gabor, did not survive.
The rest of the immediate family survived.
On the other hand-- and this is why I mentioned that our family came originally from the country-- from the wider family, very few people survived.
As you know, those who lived in Budapest survived in a larger percentage.
And those in the country died.
If I count just aunts, uncles, and cousins, I lost 39 members of my family.
Yeah, most of them.
I think that the younger ones were killed in Auschwitz immediately, as they got there, and the others died in different concentration camp.
One of them I particularly know-- my uncle who participated in the Auschwitz revolt that destroyed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the chambers.
And he was killed there by the Germans.
His name was Miklos, or in English, Nicholas.
And he was on my mother's side.
His family name was Droth, D-R-O-T-H.
My mother's maiden name.
My father was born in 1883, in a small Hungarian village called [NON-ENGLISH], and moved to Budapest in 1895.
In school, he was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, studying to become a teacher, but part-time, he worked in a bookstore.
And he liked books so much of that, although he finished his studies, he continued in the book trade and worked with an uncle who was in the book trade, and opened his own bookshop in 1901.
He was very young when he started.
And he did this as long as he was alive.
My mother was also born in the country, in a small place called [NON-ENGLISH], and they got married in 1923.
And after one year, my older brother was born.
My name was unusual in Hungary.
It's also unusual here.

And my mother, when she was carrying me, read a book from Selma Lagerlof, who is a Scandinavian writer, and was very fashionable at the time, and she decided that if I will be a boy, then my name will be Norbert because this was a hero in that book.
And this is how it happened, against strong resistance of my father, who felt that, for a Jewish boy, the name Norbert is not appropriate.
And I think he was right.
My father was very religious.
I grew up in a very religious household.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a bit more religious than most people here, in respect of-- for example, he kept his business closed on Saturdays.
And he was orthodox.
In Hungary, there were two large Jewish community-- orthodox and [INAUDIBLE].
The orthodox was very traditional, keeping all the traditions, including, for example, a very embarrassing memory of my childhood.
According to a law that was brought in Hungary in 1885 or '84, Jewish student-- if there was no Jewish school in that district where he lived-- had the right not to-- not to write on Saturdays.
And on my father's insistence, I had exercised this right, and I did not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] write anything on Saturdays.
I attended school, I listened, but I didn't write because that was not allowed according to Jewish law.
So I would say that I came from a very traditional, very strong, religious family.
No, it wasn't embarrassing to be at school.
It was embarrassing for me not to be able to write when all the others were writing.
But since this was the rule, I observed this until it was possible to observe.
I had several confrontations as a result of that, as years were becoming more and more difficult.
With-- particularly with a teacher who was of German extraction, who in 1928-- just to characterize him-- Hungarized his name from [?
Zippsel ?] to [INAUDIBLE], which is a Hungarian name, and in 1936 changed his name back to [?
Zippsel ?] as Hitler came to power.
And he decided to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make me write on Saturdays.
And, of course, I did not really obey him, and I quoted the law, that I have the right not to write.
And his method was that he put first every-- he was teaching Hungarian and German.
So every Hungarian paper that we had to write in class was on Saturdays and every German paper was on Saturdays.
So at the end of first half of the trimester, he failed me in both subjects because I didn't write any of the papers.
And, of course, this created a great deal of concern in the school.
The other teachers were upset because, up until that time, I was the best in the class.
And the director of the school, who was a reasonable man, Dr.
Palfi, ?] asked him that, what's happening?

And he told the director that he could not really give me any mark because I did not write any of the papers and he put them on the Saturdays because it's more comfortable for him to have the paper on the Saturday and to have Sunday to correct it.
The director, whose son was a classmate and a friend of mine, told him that you can have this paper written on Fridays, and you still can correct it on Sundays.
At which point he-- he had the next Hungarian paper on a Thursday.
And I failed that paper because he underlined every n and m that I wrote, that, you know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] letter was not tall enough at the beginning.
And he counted all the n's and m's in the paper, and there was about 35 of them.
So with 35 errors in a paper, nobody can pass.
So I failed that.
So I went to him and asked me if he would write me a sample n and m-- from here on, I will write it correctly.
And he did that.
And I-- next paper was a German paper on a Friday, and I failed that, too, but the n's and m's were excellent, because I wrote it exactly as he showed me.
But the s and zed were not wavy enough for him, and I failed on that.
And when I asked him to show me the s and zed, he told me that it is not his responsibility to teach me how to write at grade eight, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was that.
Nevertheless, the rest of the teachers did not allow him to fail me, particularly not in Hungarian.
I was already writing short stories at that time, and the other Hungarian teacher was very upset.
But in German I got, instead of excellent, I got the next mark.
So instead of 5, I had 4.
My Jewish education was-- first of all, in Hungary, there was no separation of the religion and state, and everybody had religious education in the school.
This was a-- a-- a subject, and it was a main subject, so you had to pass it in order to-- so that was one aspect of Jewish education.
This was mainly history and to teach me how to read or write.
The rest of my Jewish education were extra that my father organized for me, who originally did not want to send me into a regular school.
He wanted to send me into a-- a-- Jewish organization run schools, which were completely devoted to studying the Hebrew language and the religion, and the Talmud.
And this type of education, with very little--
Like a yeshiva.
But my mother, who usually had the upper hand, prevented this, and she said that we must go to regular public school, which we did.
But I had a very thorough religious education.
From the school and from the extra hours that my father organized for me.
He organized private tutors who came to the house, and also there were opportunities to learn more through our teachers in the school.
And I have taken all these opportunities.
So I would say that I had a very thorough Jewish education, by Hungarian standards.
Yes.
Bar mitzvah in Hungary was completely different from bar mitzvah here.
Here, a bar mitzvah is an-- an opportunity for the parents to invite their friends and have a big evening out of that, usually with a large expense.
In Hungary, the emphasis was to have the bar mitzvah in the synagogue, which was preceded by a six months very thorough preparation.
I had to learn far much more than my own share.
And the rest of the celebration was simply that a number of friends of mine-- and nobody from the friends of my father and mother-- were invited for an afternoon coffee.
And that was a bar mitzvah.
We did not--
At home, yeah.
You know, there was about 12 boys from the class who were invited, and that was it.
Yes.
Actually, I-- one of my best friends was not Jewish, who was sitting next to me in the Hungarian type of high school, which ran from grade 10 to-- from age 10 to age 18.
And he was sitting next to me, and we were very good friends.
He was a strong believer of the final German victory, and-- but we were good friends, and we discussed it.
I kept telling him that this is not going to happen.
And I tried to convince him that Germany's going to lose.
He tried to convince me that the new weapon that they will invent will give them world domination.

And he said that all this anti-Jewish manifestations of Hitler, once they have their victory, will cease, and he will find a solution for the Jews that will be satisfactory to all these horrible measures, trying to reassure me.
But he was a good friend because I could discuss this with him.
Vadla Joseph Vadla.
But he was completely mesmerized by the German propaganda.
Even when we met after the siege of Budapest, when the Russian occupied Budapest-- in the middle of April, the schools reopened, and we met in the schools again.
And I told him, you see?
And he told me, just wait.
Still the new weapons may come.
This was in the middle of April.
But, of course, fortunately, they did not.
We remained friends, yes.
As far as-- this conversation was after the-- after the-- after our part of the war.
The war wasn't finished yet, but as far as we were concerned, Hungary was already liberated by the Russian, so the war was over for us.

You know, he was-- he was-- You know, on one occasion, I had a temporary escape from my paramilitary unit, and on my way back, the rail was-- railway was cut by Allied bombers, and I couldn't go any further.
And I was stuck in a small suburb of Budapest, where he lived.
And it was very dangerous-- although I had some papers-- dangerous to stay in the station overnight.
So I went to his house and asked him if I could stay with him for a night, and he was very accommodating.
He said that, of course, and he will go out of his bed and just-- because I don't have that opportunity, and I sleep there.
But this only lasted 20 minutes.
When his father came in, and he told me that he wishes me all the best, but-- but not here, because it was dangerous to harbor Jews.
And he said I must leave.
And my friend was very upset.
And, you know, at age 15, he was as tall as his father, and they almost got into a fight.
He said that if I have to leave, he's going to leave.
So his-- his father told him that if he wants to do that, he doesn't need to come back, either.
So we both left.
I didn't want him to leave, but he said that he wants to leave with me.
Not just as a demonstration, but he managed to get into the factory where they worked, and we slept in the factory, which was fairly safe.
So in spite of his conviction, he was a reasonably good friend when I needed him.
And there were a few like that.
Not too many.

I would say that approximately 95% percent of the Hungarian were either too afraid or were in favor of what was happening, and I would estimate those who were brave enough to take the risk to have-- not more than 5%.
Going back a bit, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] say in the '30s, what-- what do you remember about the relationships between Jews and non-Jews in that pre-war period?
It was a-- a relationship of continuously increasing deterioration.
But we did not get extremely upset about that.
It was the accepted way of life that the Jews and Gentiles not always found understanding.
There were, of course, even in the early '30s-- because I can recall events as early as in 1934, '35, because I read the papers and everything that was related to politics early.
And there was a fascist party continuously increasing numbers-- never, mind you, passing the 24%, 25% membership.
And the relationship was separate, we lived a separate life, but there were exceptions.
There were friendships, there were inter-marriages, as well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- with a small percentage of people.
No.
It really never came up.
Nobody could believe that anything like what has happened, happened.
Even in 1944, when the Germans already occupied Hungary, we had no idea what is going on exactly.
We knew that things are not that terribly good.
But prior to that, nobody in our immediate family was thinking of leaving.

This was, as I told you, it was accepted as a way of life, and we-- looking back in history-- we always had our ups and downs as Jews during the centuries, and we did not really think that there would be any place which would be better.
That-- that was just one thing.
Even those who wanted to leave, there was no place to leave.
As you probably know, the United States and Canada was closely shut.
The only places that accepted Jewish refugees was China and some countries in South America.
But this was only after the war.
After the war.
Boy Scouts.
I was a Boy Scout from age 6 to 10.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a tremendous career as a Boy Scout, because eventually, I became the commander of our unit.
And this was a very disappointing year, because just in the middle of the year, the new law came in that Jews cannot be Boy Scouts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] except in separate Boy Scout groups.
You see, the squeeze was gradual.

The so-called Jewish laws came in, three of them, and while the first one was rather innocent, the main objection to Jews was that Jews occupy important economic positions and many important positions much higher than that percentage.
So the first Jewish law regulated that Jews cannot be any more in percentage in any given place than 20%.
And then the second one reduced it to 5%.

The 20% was basically-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'm not sure where they got this 20% percent, but this approached more the number of Jews that were approximately 600,000 in Hungary, which was about 15% of the population.
Yes.
The Jewish boys were out, and I did not join the Boy Scout movement again.
Not for any reason that I wanted to demonstrate anything, but I got interested in other things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my Boy Scout career ended at age 9 and 1/2.
Oh yes.
I have memories of Jewish holidays.
We were very strict in those.
And I went, of course, to the synagogue on each and every occasion when there was a chance to go, because my father took us.
And I had some pertinent questions fairly early.
Just, for example, to give you one-- I don't know exactly how old I was, I was around 10, 11-- when my father woke me up at 6 o'clock in the morning that we have to go to the synagogue.
The orthodox service started at 6:30.
And I told him that I'm very sleepy, maybe I could go later.
He said, this is not a good idea because I have to go because today we are praying for our life-- that we will be in the Book of Life for the next year.
So that was convincing enough, so I quickly went with him.
And then, for [NON-ENGLISH], we had to go out-- this was about an hour in Hungary, we knew when to come back.
So I walked out.
It was a beautiful fall day.
I walked up to one of the main thoroughfares in Budapest, and thousands of people were walking up and down.
In the evening, I asked my father that-- that how come these people who were walking up and down-- how do they live next year?
And he explained it to me that you know, they are of different religion, and they don't have to do that.
They have to do other things.
And there are several religions, he said, just like there are several bookstores in Budapest, he said.

Or even in Hungary, if somebody wants to buy the book, he does best if he buys it at Emil Kohn-- that was my father's name-- but there are books available at Fischer and [INAUDIBLE] and several other places.
You know, these are also books.
They are not bad books, but not as good.
So this is how he explained to me the difference between different religions-- a very practical approach.
